content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
January 15,
1993
Steven M.
Woodside
County Counsel
County of Santa Clara
70 West Hedding Street
San Jose,
CA
95110—1770
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-92—531
Dear Mr.
Woodside:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of the Santa Clara County Transit District concerning the
campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”).
1/
Since you are seeking general assistance and not
inquiring about a specific decision before you,
your letter is
considered a request for informal assistance.
2
!
OUESTION
Will the Santa Clara County Transit District make an
independent expenditure as a result of an article in its
newsletter explaining a transportation ballot measure?
CONCLUSION
Since the article in the newsletter does not expressly
advocate the election or defeat of the measure,
the Santa Clara
County Transit District will not be making an independent
expenditure.
1!
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2!
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
1-92—531
Page Two
FACTS
Prior to the November 1990 General Election, the Santa Clara
County Transportation District (“District”)
published an article
in its newsletter concerning the “Measure A,” the transportation
initiative on the November 1992 ballot in Santa Clara County.
The
article informed its readers that Measure
A would extend the
current half-cent sales tax commencing April 1,
1995 for highway
and county expressway improvements,
as well as for rail transit
expansion and operation.
The article further provided that should
the measure pass, the Local Transportation Authority would
administer the revenues and oversee expansion of transit lines.
Readers were directed to contact the proponents of the measure,
“Citizens Coalition for Traffic Relief” if they had additional
questions.
This article was contained in a newsletter distributed
on buses to its riders,
and published in the employee’s
newsletter.
At the time the article was first printed,
there was not in
existence a committee opposed to the ballot measure.
Shortly
after the first distribution of the newsletter,
such a committee
was formed.
Before the District was allowed to distribute the
newsletter further,
you faxed us
a copy of the article and asked
if the article constituted an “independent expenditure” to the
“Citizens Coalition for Traffic Relief,” the committee supporting
the ballot measure.
You were advised at that time that the
article did not constitute an “independent expenditure.”
You
followed up that conversation with this request for written
advice.
ANALYSIS
Section 82013 of the Act provides:
“Committee” means any person or combination of
persons who directly or indirectly does any of the
following:
(a)
Receives contributions totaling one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more in a
calendar year.
(b)
Makes independent expenditures
totaling one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more
in a calendar year;
or
(c)
Makes contributions totaling ten
thousand dollars
($10,000)
or more in a
calendar year to or at the behest of
candidates or committees.
A person or combination of persons that becomes a committee
shall retain its status as a committee until such time as that
status is terminated pursuant to Section 84214.


Advice File No.
1—92—531
Page Three
The Act defines “independent expenditure” as:
[A]n expenditure made by any person in
connection with a communication which expressly
advocates the election or defeat of a clearly
identified candidate or the qualification, passage
or defeat of a clearly identified measure,
or taken
as a whole and in context,
unambiguously urges a
particular result in an election but which is not
made to or at the behest of the affected candidate
or committee.
Section 82031.
“Expenditure” includes any monetary or non—
monetary payment made by any person.
.
. that is used
for communications which expressly advocate the
nomination,
election or defeat of a clearly
identified candidate or candidates,
or the
qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly
identified ballot measure.
“Clearly identified,” as it applies to a ballot
measure means:
(C)
A measure that has qualified to
be placed on the ballot is clearly
identified if the communication states a
proposition numbered, official title or
popular name associated with the measure.
In addition,
the measure is clearly
identified if the communication refers to
the subject matter of the measure and
either states that the measure is before
the people for a vote or,
taken as a
whole and in context,
unambiguously
refers to the measure.
***
(2)
A communication “expressly
advocates” the nomination, election or defeat
of a candidate or the qualification,
passage
or defeat of a measure if it contains express
words of advocacy such as “vote for,” “elect,”
“support,” “cast your ballot,” “vote against,”
“defeat,” “reject,” “sign petitions for” or
otherwise refers to a clearly identified
candidate or measure so that the
communication,
taken as a whole,
unambiguously
urges a particular result in an election.
Regulation 18225(b)
(Emphasis
added).


Advice File No.
1-92—531
Page Four
The communication in the District’s newsletter does not
specifically urge the readers to vote for or against the ballot
measure.
The communication does not expressly advocate the
passage or defeat of the measure by containing words of advocacy
such as “vote for,” “support,” “cast your ballot,” or “vote
against.”
It is a communication which informs the electorate of
the projects which would be affected by the ballot measure.
The
mere fact that the address and telephone number of the proponent’s
committee is included, without also providing the address and
telephone number of the opponent’s committee,
does not expressly
advocate the passage of the measure.
The Santa Clara County Transit District will not make an
independent expenditure supporting the passage of “Measure A” the
transportation initiative on the November 1992 ballot in Santa
Clara County.
3
!
I hope this has been helpful to you.
If there is anything
further you wish to discuss, please feel free to contact this
office at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By:
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
JET! j t
3!
There may be the additional question of whether such an
expenditure by the District would be considered a legal
expenditure. (,
e.g.
Penal Code Section 424;
Stanson v.
Mott
(1976)
17 Cal.
3d 206.
Penal Code Section 424 which deals with
the misuse of public moneys,
are outside the jurisdiction of the
Commission.
Penal Code Section 424 is interpreted and enforced by
the district attorney and the Attorney General.

Advice File No.
1-92—531

independent expenditure as a result of an article in its

the Santa Clara
County Transit District will not be making an independent
expenditure.
